The Light and Shadow of Frontend Engineering: Think It Through Before You Switch In

We're in an age of information overload — knowledge is easier to get, but that also means more time spent thinking, weighing, comparing, deciding. Not least because "become a developer" is a really popular career switch right now.
What do I mean by "light and shadow"? Light = the upside of frontend engineering. Shadow = the downside. Before getting into specifics, let me talk about why you're reading this.

If you're here, you're probably thinking about whether to become a frontend developer. Career switchers especially — your background varies wildly. Some come from design. Some from marketing. Some from totally unrelated fields. But you all have one thing in common — you want to become a frontend developer.
Why do you want to become a frontend developer?
I've mentored quite a few career switchers (and CS grads too). I've asked this question and dug into the reasons. The most common ones:
Just need to face a computer
The reasons are all over the map. I'll only focus on a few — the ones I think are most important and worth real reflection. Let's go through them.
Why is developer salary usually high?
Quick econ — supply and demand. Two scenarios:
When demand outstrips supply, the demand side will pay more to get the supply. That's part of why developer salary ceilings are high.

Second reason — the field moves fast (very fast). For frontend specifically, a minor shift every six months and a major shift every two years is normal. So developers are forced to be in a constant state of learning.

Third reason, slightly different — when you've actually built strong skills, your ceiling jumps further. Companies will work harder to keep and attract good engineers, so they pay above-market for proven talent.
Now the interesting questions to sit with:
What happens when knowledge becomes easier and easier to access?
I'll leave that one for you to think about.

Job opportunities are interesting. Why does developer specifically have so many? My take — it's one of the least restrictive professions. Developers work in basically every industry. And it's one of the least geographically constrained — you can find work nearly anywhere.
But there's a catch. Once you remove geographic limits, what comes next?
. If you want to work internationally, you've likely already accepted you'll need decent English. If you haven't thought about it, time to start.
Beyond language — the other thing people romanticize is

The biggest issue with remote isn't tech, it's communication. In-person, you read facial expressions and tone, and clarify questions in real time. Remote, you're mostly typing. So the question becomes — can your writing convey what you mean accurately?
When you can't understand what someone else meant, what do you do? "Just hop on a call" — sure, but you having time doesn't mean they have time. The world doesn't revolve around you.
And remote teams are often spread across timezones. If your problem is urgent — who's awake? A lot of things you have to figure out yourself instead of waiting for someone.
This one's funny. I've asked plenty of people what they mean by "high flexibility". Summary of what I hear:
"I can work from anywhere, anytime, instead of being tied to a specific location."
For a frontend dev with a laptop and stable internet, you can work pretty much anywhere — which on paper sounds more flexible than most jobs. But have you actually thought it through?

What does "flexibility" actually mean? Setting other professions aside — even just for developers: you carry your laptop everywhere, you're constantly checking your phone, and even on vacation you're hunting for stable Wi-Fi. Is that really flexibility?
It gets interesting when you think about it:
When you're out, your phone buzzes — do you want to look?
When you're out, your phone buzzes — do you want to respond?
When you're out, your phone buzzes — do you want to handle it?
Is that really the kind of freedom you wanted? Do you actually like that life? Sit with it.
You should know the costs of freedom, not just its upside.
This one's more direct. People usually think "facing a computer" is better because their current job means facing customers all day, and anything involving people gets complicated. Fair enough.
Developers do face computers most of the time, but the core value of the work is solving problems. And solving problems involves…
"Can't I just write code and solve problems quietly?"

Sure — but don't you talk to your coworkers and manager? Don't you need to clarify requirements? Building a system means coordinating with people about what to build and how to divide work. You can't avoid that.

Honestly, requirement gathering is half the job. You'll hear wild ideas from stakeholders, then have to translate them and explain in terms they understand. That communication is what makes shipping anything possible.
Don't underestimate any of this. As soon as you hear any of these phrases, brace yourself:
"This is just a small feature, just…"
"This is just a minor tweak, just…"
You will lose a whole week clarifying that "small" requirement. Guaranteed.
Because customers and managers think in the abstract. They have a lot of fantasy about what being a developer is:
"I just need to say what I want — I'm paying."
Reflecting on this — do you really "just face a computer"? The moment
enter, things get complicated. Don't assume you're going to be alone with your editor.
If your current job is genuinely physically taxing, switching to dev is a reasonable choice — at minimum it's better than where you are.
But honest take — being a dev isn't gentle on the body either:
Long hours staring at a screen → eye strain
Long hours sitting → back, neck, shoulder issues
New tech constantly → continuous learning pressure
If your current job is literally shortening your lifespan, switching to dev is a real win. If it's not, switching to dev might still hurt you over time.

And — compared to CS grads, your starting salary will probably be lower. You don't have the credential, your job history isn't in the field, so you have to put in N times the effort to catch up. Otherwise what did all those years of CS school amount to for them?
All of this is worth sitting with.
What you'll face after becoming a frontend dev
I've covered a lot of common fantasies vs. reality. Now — what's the day-to-day after you make the switch?
One thing I want you to internalize — treat yourself like

. Your "customers" are your manager and exec. Your job is solving their problems. Don't think of yourself as "tech industry" — because you're not going to spend 8 hours a day coding. Maybe 20% of your time is code; the other 80% is communication.

. Funny right? Developer of all things has information anxiety as a core challenge. In frontend specifically, every six to twelve months there's a new framework, a new pattern. So you're forced to stay learning. There's no escape.
Please stop releasing new things, my brain can't take it
I've mentored students with "information anxiety syndrome". The fix isn't complicated, but a lot of people struggle with it:
Looks simple, hard to do — especially with social media constantly in your face. Worth holding onto. And
(generative AI) has made this worse, not better.
Last one and important — accept that

is part of it. Of every job I know, developer is the easiest one to slip into overtime. Plenty of companies try hard not to require it, but realistically? It happens. You're solving problems, and problems come with unexpected sub-problems. Solving those takes overtime.
Plus business reality — your manager or exec may straight up ask you to work late, because the product needs to ship by a specific date.

Personally, I once worked from 9 AM to 4 AM the next morning, then had to be back at 8 AM, because we were trying to ship a system on time. Missing that deadline would've cost the company at least a million NTD — and a project tanking because of you is bad for your job security.

Developers are like infants — every day, facing new things.
Always learning, always growing, always facing challenges. That's the gig.

Coding is like Lego. Developers are toddlers trying to build the structure their parents (or themselves) asked for. Along the way, you hit problems. Those problems are your challenges. Facing them is your growth.
"How do I build with Lego well?"
How do you build flexibly? That's another skill you'll have to learn —
. But that's a big topic, so I won't go into it here. Just know:
Becoming a developer doesn't mean you're done. There's a lot more to learn.
One last critical thing — no matter what kind of developer you become, you
. Most docs are in English. So you have to learn to read them. Bugs, features you haven't used — answers are in the official docs, sometimes in the Issues.

Google Translate is helpful, but reading the original English is often better — translations of technical terms can be weird, and different translations across languages add confusion. So in addition to your learning plan, get comfortable reading English docs.

Why are docs so important? Honestly, I have to gripe — I've written articles and personal notes for students I mentor, and many of the questions they ask are already answered in those posts. But they still ask.

. They ask other people instead of looking. That's a habit you really want to break — because you have no guarantee anyone will respond, and no guarantee their answer is right. Learn to search instead of ask. Most answers are in the docs already, if you just look.
Why did I write this? Because I want anyone considering a switch into dev to know what they're walking into — not just the romanticized version.
If someone makes the dev life look effortless, great for them — but you and I don't know how they got there.

I want this post to give you a heads-up before you walk in. If you just want to try it to see whether dev interests you — go for it, no problem. You only really know if you like something by doing it.

But if you're switching careers for real, I want you to think it through. This field genuinely isn't as nice as it looks from the outside, and you'll have to put in a lot. CS grads have an inherent head start; you're starting behind, and have to put in more time and effort to catch up.

Either way — I don't want you switching just because your current job is bad this week. I want you to think it through and decide. If you've thought it through and you're walking in — I genuinely hope it works out.

We're in an age where information is everywhere and easy to grab — which means we have to spend more energy thinking, comparing, and deciding. Especially when "becoming a developer" is the trendy career switch. This post is the honest version of what frontend engineering looks like — the upside (salary ceiling, opportunities, flexibility) and the downside (information anxiety, communication load, the reality of "remote" and "freedom") — so you can make an informed decision.
